explanations.5: The left number (8000) is the port on your computer; the right number (80) is the port inside the container—traffic coming to your machine on 8000 gets forwarded to the container's port 80.
explanations.7: This mounts your local main.py file into the container at /code/main.py so changes you make locally are immediately visible inside the running container.
explanations.13: The ':ro' at the end means 'read-only'—the container can see these files but cannot modify them, which protects your source code from accidental changes.
explanations.14: This creates a persistent storage volume named 'maven-cache' that survives even after the container stops, so Maven doesn't have to re-download dependencies every time.
explanations.17: Normally this container would run Maven directly, but we're replacing that with a bash shell so we can chain multiple commands together using pipes (the | symbol on line 29).
explanations.21: The '>' character means the command continues on the next lines—it's a way to write a really long command in a readable format without actually pressing Enter.
explanations.28: '2>&1' merges error messages (stderr) and normal output (stdout) into one stream so the grep filters catch all the noise equally.
